what: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: Tidy N-Body Orbital Mechanics R

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: Installation Physics API Reference Examples Unstable Orbits Three-Body Problem 3D Plotting Custom Visualization ggplot2 Custom Visualization plotly Built-Physical Constants License orbitr lightweight N-body gravitational physics engine built R ecosystem. Simulate planetary orbits, binary star systems, chaotic three-body problems lines pipe-friendly code. hood ships compiled C++ acceleration engine via Rcpp falls back gracefully fully vectorized pure-R implementation.
code: library(orbitr)  ##  ## Attaching package: 'orbitr'  ## The following object is masked from 'package:stats': ##  ##     simulate  create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_sun + distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_earth + speed_moon) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365) |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth") |>   plot_orbits()

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("daverosenman/orbitr")

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: Every body system attracts every body according Newton’s Law Universal Gravitation. body j, net acceleration due bodies k : $$\vec{}\_j = \sum\_{k \neq j} \frac{G \\ m\_k}{r\_{jk}^2} \\ \hat{r}\_{jk}$$ rjk = |r⃗k − r⃗j| distance two bodies r̂jk unit vector pointing j toward k.

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: Gravity alone pull every body straight toward every body. prevents colliding initial velocity — sideways motion turns free-fall curved orbit. reason Moon doesn’t crash Earth: ’s falling toward us constantly, ’s also moving sideways fast enough keeps missing. call add_body(), vx, vy, vz parameters set initial velocity. balance speed distance determines shape orbit. given distance r central mass M, circular orbit velocity : $$v\_{\text{circ}} = \sqrt{\frac{G \\ M}{r}}$$ body’s speed exactly matches , traces perfect circle. Faster orbit stretches ellipse (escapes entirely v≥v_circ2v \geq v\_{\text{circ}} \sqrt{2}). Slower orbit drops tighter ellipse dips closer central body. zero velocity, body falls straight — orbit .

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: two bodies pass close, r → 0 acceleration diverges toward infinity. well-known numerical problem N-body codes. orbitr offers optional softening length ε regularizes potential: r_soft=r2+ε2r\_{\text{soft}} = \sqrt{r^2 + \varepsilon^2} softening enabled, close encounters produce large finite forces instead blowing NaN. Set softening = 0 (default) exact Newtonian gravity, try something like softening = 1e4 (10 km) dense systems.

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: simulate() offers three methods stepping system forward time. operate 3D Cartesian coordinates.

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: second-order symplectic integrator. conserves energy long timescales, making gold standard orbital mechanics. Orbits stay closed stable indefinitely. $$\vec{x}\_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{x}\_t + \vec{v}\_t \\ \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} \vec{}\_t \\ \Delta t^2$$ v→_t+Δt=v→_t+12(→_t+→_t+Δt)Δt\vec{v}\_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{v}\_t + \tfrac{1}{2} \left( \vec{}\_t + \vec{}\_{t+\Delta t} \right) \Delta t position advanced first, acceleration recalculated new position, finally velocity updated using average old new accelerations. requires two acceleration evaluations per step (main cost), payoff stability enormous.

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: first-order symplectic method. updates velocity first, uses new velocity update position. small reordering prevents systematic energy drift plagues standard Euler: v⃗t + Δt = v⃗t + ⃗t Δt x⃗t + Δt = x⃗t + v⃗t + Δt *Δ**t* Faster Verlet (one acceleration evaluation per step) less accurate. Good quick previews.

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: classical textbook method. Position velocity updated using values current time step: x⃗t + Δt = x⃗t + v⃗t Δt v⃗t + Δt = v⃗t + ⃗t Δt artificially pumps energy system, causing orbits spiral outward time. Included primarily educational comparison — use Verlet real work.

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: inner acceleration loop computational bottleneck N-body simulation. orbitr ships compiled C++ kernel (via Rcpp) computes O(n2) pairwise interactions tight nested loop. package installed source working C++ toolchain, simulate() automatically dispatches engine. compiled code isn’t available, falls back vectorized R implementation uses matrix outer products — still fast, C++ path significantly faster systems many bodies. can control use_cpp argument:
code: # Force the pure-R engine (useful for debugging or benchmarking) simulate(system, use_cpp = FALSE)

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: Initializes empty orbital simulation. gravitational constant G set applies bodies added later. Set G = 0 zero-gravity (inertia-) environment. Returns orbit_system S3 object.
code: # Standard gravity (G = 6.6743e-11) universe <- create_system()  # Stronger gravity (10x) universe <- create_system(G = 6.6743e-10)   # Zero gravity sandbox universe <- create_system(G = 0)

what: add_body(system, id, mass, x, y, z, vx, vy, vz)
title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: Adds celestial body system. Position (x, y, z) meters, velocity (vx, vy, vz) meters per second. default 0, placing body origin rest. Piping-friendly: returns updated orbit_system.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = 5.97e24) |>   add_body("Moon", mass = 7.34e22, x = 3.84e8, vy = 1022)

what: simulate(system, time_step, duration, method, softening, use_cpp)
title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: core engine. Propagates system forward time returns full trajectory tidy tibble. Returns tibble columns: time, id, mass, x, y, z, vx, vy, vz.

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: Transforms positions velocities chosen body sits origin every time step. go heliocentric view geocentric one, example.
code: # View the Moon's orbit from Earth's perspective sim |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth") |>   plot_orbits()  # Remove Earth from the plot entirely sim |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth", keep_center = FALSE) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: smart plotting dispatcher automatically chooses 2D 3D visualization. body non-zero Z positions (three_d = TRUE), renders interactive 3D plot using plotly. Otherwise produces 2D trajectory map (x vs y) using ggplot2 coord_equal(). Returns ggplot object (2D) plotly HTML widget (3D).

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: Generates interactive 3D visualization using plotly. can click drag rotate, scroll zoom, hover trajectories see body names timestamps. Uses aspectmode = "data" preserve proportions circular orbits look circular 3D space. Requires plotly package. Returns plotly HTML widget.

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: standard 28-day lunar orbit. One-hour time steps.
code: library(orbitr)  create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon, x = distance_earth_moon, vy = speed_moon) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: full year daily time steps.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   simulate(time_step = 86400, duration = 86400 * 365) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: orbitr uses N-body gravity, nested hierarchies require special setup. Piggyback Moon’s initial conditions onto Earth’s using simple vector addition:
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_sun + distance_earth_moon,   # Earth's X + lunar orbital radius            vy = speed_earth + speed_moon) |>               # Earth's speed + Moon's orbital speed   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: three-body plot heliocentric (Sun center). see Moon’s path Earth’s perspective, pipe results shift_reference_frame():
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_sun + distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_earth + speed_moon) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365) |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth") |>   plot_orbits()

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: Use method argument see different integrators behave long simulations:  ’ll see Verlet traces clean closed ellipse, Euler-Cromer stays close drifts slightly, standard Euler spirals outward pumps energy orbit.
code: library(dplyr)  ##  ## Attaching package: 'dplyr'  ## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': ##  ##     filter, lag  ## The following objects are masked from 'package:base': ##  ##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union  system <- create_system() |>   add_body("Star", mass = 1e30) |>   add_body("Planet", mass = 1e24, x = 1e11, vy = 30000)  verlet <- simulate(system, time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365, method = "verlet") |>   mutate(method = "Velocity Verlet")  euler_cromer <- simulate(system, time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365, method = "euler_cromer") |>   mutate(method = "Euler-Cromer")  euler <- simulate(system, time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365, method = "euler") |>   mutate(method = "Standard Euler")  bind_rows(verlet, euler_cromer, euler) |>   filter(id == "Planet") |>   ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = y, color = method)) +   ggplot2::geom_path(alpha = 0.7) +   ggplot2::coord_equal() +   ggplot2::theme_minimal()

what: A Stable Binary Star System with a Circumbinary Planet
title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: Two equal-mass stars orbit common center mass planet orbits pair far away. key stability placing planet well outside binary orbit — general rule thumb least 3–4 times star separation.
code: # Two stars, each 1 solar mass, separated by 0.5 AU # They orbit their barycenter (the origin) in a circle star_sep   <- 0.5 * distance_earth_sun   # 0.5 AU apart star_r     <- star_sep / 2               # each is 0.25 AU from center star_v     <- sqrt(mass_sun * 6.6743e-11 / (4 * star_r))  # circular binary velocity  # Planet at 3 AU from the barycenter — well outside the binary planet_r   <- 3 * distance_earth_sun planet_v   <- sqrt(2 * mass_sun * 6.6743e-11 / planet_r)  # circular velocity around total mass  create_system() |>   add_body("Star A", mass = mass_sun,   x =  star_r, vy =  star_v) |>   add_body("Star B", mass = mass_sun,   x = -star_r, vy = -star_v) |>   add_body("Planet", mass = mass_earth, x = planet_r, vy = planet_v) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365 * 3) |>   plot_orbits()

what: Unstable Orbits and the Three-Body Problem
title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: start plugging random masses velocities, ’ll quickly discover configurations wildly unstable. isn’t bug — ’s physics. Stable orbits exception, rule. two-body system, stability relatively easy achieve: give smaller body right velocity right distance traces clean ellipse forever. moment add third body, things get chaotic. three-body problem general closed-form solution — small differences initial conditions lead dramatically different outcomes, including bodies flung system entirely. ’s example: three equal-mass stars arranged triangle slightly asymmetric velocities. starts looking like interesting dance, asymmetry compounds eventually one stars get ejected:  actually happens real stellar dynamics — close three-body encounters star clusters frequently eject one star high velocity remaining two settle tighter binary. process called gravitational slingshot ejection. simulations producing messy, diverging trajectories, things check assuming something wrong: Velocity high low. given distance r central mass M, circular orbit speed v=GM/rv = \sqrt{GM/r}. Deviating significantly produces eccentric orbits escape trajectories. Bodies close together. Close encounters produce extreme accelerations can blow numerically. Try increasing softening using smaller time_step. Three bodies. Chaos natural state N-body systems. stable examples README carefully tuned — don’t expect random configurations behave. Time step large. bodies move significant fraction orbital radius single step, integrator can’t track orbit accurately. Try halving time_step see result changes. built-constants examples orbitr designed give stable starting points. can tweak parameters watch system responds — ’s real intuition orbital mechanics comes .
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Star A", mass = 1e30, x = 1e11, y = 0, vx = 0, vy = 15000) |>   add_body("Star B", mass = 1e30, x = -5e10, y = 8.66e10, vx = -12990, vy = -7500) |>   add_body("Star C", mass = 1e30, x = -5e10, y = -8.66e10, vx = 14000, vy = -8000) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365 * 10) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: simulations orbitr run full 3D — every body always x, y, z coordinates. motion happens XY plane (.e., z = 0 vz = 0 every body), plot_orbits() produces static 2D ggplot2 chart. moment body non-zero Z motion, plot_orbits() automatically switches interactive 3D plotly visualization — code changes needed. can also force 3D rendering planar data three_d = TRUE, can useful want interactive rotation zoom capabilities even flat system.

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: Moon’s real orbit inclined 5° ecliptic. can approximate giving Moon small vz component:  vz non-zero, plot_orbits() detects 3D motion returns interactive plotly widget. can drag rotate, scroll zoom, hover see timestamps.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_moon * cos(5 * pi / 180),            vz = speed_moon * sin(5 * pi / 180)) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: plot_orbits() plot_orbits_3d() convenience functions quick trajectory plots — ’re designed get useful visualization one line can focus setting physics. real power orbitr simulate() returns standard tidy tibble. can use ggplot2, plotly, visualization tool directly output. ’s raw output looks like: row one body one point time. Every column available plotting, filtering, analysis. Since just tibble, full power dplyr ggplot2 disposal. example, Earth-Moon system, plot_orbits() shows overlapping circles bodies orbit shared barycenter roughly scale. useful visualization might plot body’s distance barycenter time:  plot Moon’s path relative Earth color gradient showing passage time:
code: sim <- create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon, x = distance_earth_moon, vy = speed_moon) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28)  sim  ## # A tibble: 1,346 × 9 ##    id       mass          x           y     z      vx          vy    vz  time ##    <chr>   <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> ##  1 Earth 5.97e24         0         0        0   0        0            0     0 ##  2 Moon  7.34e22 384400000         0        0   0     1022            0     0 ##  3 Earth 5.97e24       215.        0        0   0.119    0.000571     0  3600 ##  4 Moon  7.34e22 384382520.  3679200        0  -9.71  1022.           0  3600 ##  5 Earth 5.97e24       860.        4.11     0   0.239    0.00229      0  7200 ##  6 Moon  7.34e22 384330083.  7358065.       0 -19.4   1022.           0  7200 ##  7 Earth 5.97e24      1934.       16.5      0   0.358    0.00514      0 10800 ##  8 Moon  7.34e22 384242692. 11036262.       0 -29.1   1022.           0 10800 ##  9 Earth 5.97e24      3438.       41.1      0   0.477    0.00914      0 14400 ## 10 Moon  7.34e22 384120357. 14713454.       0 -38.8   1021.           0 14400 ## # ℹ 1,336 more rows library(ggplot2)  sim |>   dplyr::mutate(r = sqrt(x^2 + y^2)) |>   ggplot(aes(x = time / 86400, y = r, color = id)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1) +   labs(     title = "Distance from Barycenter Over Time",     x = "Time (days)",     y = "Distance (m)",     color = "Body"   ) +   theme_minimal() sim |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth", keep_center = FALSE) |>   ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y, color = time / 86400)) +   geom_path(linewidth = 1.2) +   scale_color_viridis_c(name = "Day") +   coord_equal() +   labs(title = "Lunar Orbit (Earth-Centered)", x = "X (m)", y = "Y (m)") +   theme_minimal()

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: Just plot_orbits() quick convenience 2D work, plot_orbits_3d() quick convenience 3D. intentionally simple — get useful plot one line can focus physics, formatting. need control, simulation tibble works just well plotly ggplot2. example, color trajectories speed rather body, add markers start end orbit:  point ggplot2: simulate() returns standard tibble, full access plotly’s API anything built-plotting functions don’t cover.
code: library(plotly)  ## Warning: package 'plotly' was built under R version 4.5.3  ##  ## Attaching package: 'plotly'  ## The following object is masked from 'package:ggplot2': ##  ##     last_plot  ## The following object is masked from 'package:stats': ##  ##     filter  ## The following object is masked from 'package:graphics': ##  ##     layout  sim <- create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_moon * cos(5 * pi / 180),            vz = speed_moon * sin(5 * pi / 180)) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28)  sim <- sim |>   dplyr::mutate(speed = sqrt(vx^2 + vy^2 + vz^2))  plot_ly() |>   add_trace(     data = dplyr::filter(sim, id == "Moon"),     x = ~x, y = ~y, z = ~z,     type = 'scatter3d', mode = 'lines',     line = list(       width = 5,       color = ~speed,       colorscale = 'Viridis',       showscale = TRUE,       colorbar = list(title = "Speed (m/s)")     ),     name = "Moon"   ) |>   add_trace(     data = dplyr::filter(sim, id == "Earth"),     x = ~x, y = ~y, z = ~z,     type = 'scatter3d', mode = 'lines',     line = list(width = 3, color = 'gray'),     name = "Earth"   ) |>   layout(     title = "Lunar Orbit Around Earth",     showlegend = FALSE,     scene = list(       xaxis = list(title = 'X (m)'),       yaxis = list(title = 'Y (m)'),       zaxis = list(title = 'Z (m)'),       aspectmode = "data"     )   )

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: orbitr ships set real-world masses, distances, orbital speeds don’t Google every time. values SI units (kg, meters, m/s). means Earth-Moon example can written :
code: library(orbitr)  # Masses mass_sun          # 1.989e30 kg mass_earth        # 5.972e24 kg mass_moon         # 7.342e22 kg mass_mars         # 6.417e23 kg mass_jupiter      # 1.898e27 kg mass_saturn       # 5.683e26 kg mass_venus        # 4.867e24 kg mass_mercury      # 3.301e23 kg  # Orbital distances (semi-major axes) distance_earth_sun    # 1.496e11 m  (~149.6 million km) distance_earth_moon   # 3.844e8  m  (~384,400 km) distance_mars_sun     # 2.279e11 m distance_jupiter_sun  # 7.785e11 m distance_venus_sun    # 1.082e11 m distance_mercury_sun  # 5.791e10 m  # Mean orbital speeds speed_earth       # 29,780 m/s speed_moon        #  1,022 m/s speed_mars        # 24,070 m/s speed_jupiter     # 13,060 m/s speed_venus       # 35,020 m/s speed_mercury     # 47,360 m/s create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon, x = distance_earth_moon, vy = speed_moon) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: Orbital distances truly constant — every orbit ellipse, separation two bodies changes continuously throughout revolution. values provided semi-major axes: average closest approach (periapsis) farthest point (apoapsis). semi-major axis single characteristic length scale elliptical orbit. determines orbital period via Kepler’s Third Law, paired mean orbital speed, produces near-circular trajectory closely approximates real orbit. example, Earth-Sun distance varies 147.1 million km January (perihelion) 152.1 million km July (aphelion). semi-major axis 149.6 million km sits right middle gives correct one-year orbital period. want elliptical orbit instead, start body periapsis faster--mean velocity, apoapsis slower one.

title: Tidy Orbital Mechanics and N-Body Physics Simulation
text: MIT

what: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
text: function introduces new celestial physical body `orbit_system`. must provide unique identifier mass. default, body placed origin (0, 0, 0) zero initial velocity unless specified.

title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
code: add_body(system, id, mass, x = 0, y = 0, z = 0, vx = 0, vy = 0, vz = 0)

title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
text: system `orbit_system` object created `create_system()`. id unique character string identify body (e.g., "Earth", "Apollo"). mass mass object kilograms. x Initial X-axis position meters (default 0). y Initial Y-axis position meters (default 0). z Initial Z-axis position meters (default 0). vx Initial velocity along X-axis meters per second (default 0). vy Initial velocity along Y-axis meters per second (default 0). vz Initial velocity along Z-axis meters per second (default 0).

title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
text: updated `orbit_system` object containing newly added body.

title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ my_universe <- create_system() |>   add_body(id = "Earth", mass = 5.97e24) |>   add_body(id = "Moon", mass = 7.34e22, x = 3.84e8, vy = 1022) } # }

what: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
text: Sets foundational data structure orbital simulation. Universal N-body gravity automatically integrated system using specified gravitational constant.

title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
code: create_system(G = 6.6743e-11)

title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
text: G gravitational constant. Defaults standard physics (6.6743e-11). simulate zero-gravity environment (inertia ), set `G = 0`.

title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
text: empty `orbit_system` object ready bodies added.

title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
code: # Creates a system with standard gravity my_universe <- create_system()  # Creates a universe with 10x stronger gravity heavy_universe <- create_system(G = 6.6743e-10)  # Creates a zero-gravity sandbox floating_universe <- create_system(G = 0)

title: Physical Constants for Orbital Mechanics — physical_constants
text: `mass_sun`: Mass Sun (1.989 x 10^30 kg). Source: IAU 2015 nominal solar mass. `mass_earth`: Mass Earth (5.972 x 10^24 kg). Source: IAU 2015 nominal Earth mass. `mass_moon`: Mass Moon (7.342 x 10^22 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_mars`: Mass Mars (6.417 x 10^23 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_jupiter`: Mass Jupiter (1.898 x 10^27 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_saturn`: Mass Saturn (5.683 x 10^26 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_venus`: Mass Venus (4.867 x 10^24 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_mercury`: Mass Mercury (3.301 x 10^23 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `distance_earth_sun`: Semi-major axis Earth's orbit around Sun   (1.496 x 10^11 m, ~149.6 million km). Earth's actual distance varies   ~147.1 million km (perihelion) ~152.1 million km (aphelion). `distance_earth_moon`: Semi-major axis Moon's orbit around Earth   (3.844 x 10^8 m, ~384,400 km). Moon's actual distance varies   ~363,300 km (perigee) ~405,500 km (apogee). `distance_mars_sun`: Semi-major axis Mars's orbit around Sun   (2.279 x 10^11 m, ~227.9 million km). Mars notably eccentric orbit   (e = 0.093), ranging ~206.7 million km ~249.2 million km. `distance_jupiter_sun`: Semi-major axis Jupiter's orbit around Sun   (7.785 x 10^11 m, ~778.5 million km). `distance_venus_sun`: Semi-major axis Venus's orbit around Sun   (1.082 x 10^11 m, ~108.2 million km). Venus circular orbit   planet (e = 0.007). `distance_mercury_sun`: Semi-major axis Mercury's orbit around Sun   (5.791 x 10^10 m, ~57.9 million km). Mercury eccentric planetary   orbit (e = 0.206), ranging ~46.0 million km ~69.8 million km. `speed_earth`: Mean orbital speed Earth around Sun (29,780 m/s). `speed_moon`: Mean orbital speed Moon around Earth (1,022 m/s). `speed_mars`: Mean orbital speed Mars around Sun (24,070 m/s). `speed_jupiter`: Mean orbital speed Jupiter around Sun (13,060 m/s). `speed_venus`: Mean orbital speed Venus around Sun (35,020 m/s). `speed_mercury`: Mean orbital speed Mercury around Sun (47,360 m/s).

title: Physical Constants for Orbital Mechanics — physical_constants
text: Orbital distances truly constant. Every orbit ellipse, separation two bodies changes continuously. distances provided **semi-major axes** — average closest approach (periapsis) farthest point (apoapsis). semi-major axis single characteristic length scale elliptical orbit: determines orbital period via Kepler's Third Law, paired circular velocity distance, produces near-circular orbit closely approximates real trajectory. example, Earth-Sun distance varies 147.1 million km (perihelion January) 152.1 million km (aphelion July). semi-major axis 149.598 million km sits right middle gives correct orbital period one year.

title: Plot Orbital Trajectories (Smart 2D/3D Dispatch) — plot_orbits
text: Plot Orbital Trajectories (Smart 2D/3D Dispatch)

title: Plot Orbital Trajectories (Smart 2D/3D Dispatch) — plot_orbits
code: plot_orbits(sim_data, three_d = FALSE)

title: Plot Orbital Trajectories (Smart 2D/3D Dispatch) — plot_orbits
text: sim_data tibble output `simulate()` three_d Logical. TRUE, forces 3D plot even 2D data.

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d
text: Generates interactive 3D visualization orbital system using plotly. can click, drag rotate, scroll zoom trajectories.

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d
code: plot_orbits_3d(sim_data)

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d
text: sim_data tibble containing simulation output `simulate()`.

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d
text: plotly HTML widget displaying 3D orbits.

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = 5.972e24) |>   add_body("Moon", mass = 7.342e22, x = 3.844e8, vy = 1022, vz = 150) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 30) |>   plot_orbits_3d() } # }

what: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
text: Recalculates positions velocities bodies relative specific target body. effectively "anchors camera" chosen body, placing origin (0, 0, 0) time steps.

title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
code: shift_reference_frame(sim_data, center_id, keep_center = TRUE)

title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
text: sim_data tidy `tibble` containing output `simulate()`. center_id character string ID body use new origin. keep_center Logical. central body remain dataset (0 coordinates) removed? Default `TRUE`.

title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
text: tidy `tibble` updated `x`, `y`, `z`, `vx`, `vy`, `vz` columns.

title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate Sun-Earth-Moon orbit_data <- create_system() |>   add_body("Sun", mass = 1.989e30) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = 5.97e24, x = 1.496e11, vy = 29780) |>   add_body("Moon", mass = 7.34e22, x = 1.496e11 + 3.84e8, vy = 29780 + 1022) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365)  # Shift view to Earth and plot orbit_data |>   shift_reference_frame(center_id = "Earth") |>   plot_orbits() } # }

what: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate
title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate
text: Propagates physical state `orbit_system` time using numerical integration. engine supports multiple mathematical methods, defaulting energy-conserving Velocity Verlet algorithm ensure highly stable orbital trajectories.

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate
code: simulate(   system,   time_step = 60,   duration = 86400,   method = "verlet",   softening = 0,   use_cpp = TRUE )

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate
text: system `orbit_system` object created `create_system()`. time_step time increment per frame seconds (default 60s). duration Total simulation time seconds (default 86400s / 1 day). method numerical integration method: "verlet" (default), "euler_cromer", "euler". softening small distance (meters) added prevent numerical singularities bodies pass close . gravitational distance computed `sqrt(r^2 + softening^2)` instead `r`. Default 0 (softening). value like 1e4 (10 km) reasonable planetary simulations. use_cpp Logical. `TRUE` (default), uses compiled C++ acceleration engine better performance. Falls back vectorized R C++ code available.

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate
text: tidy `tibble` containing physical state (time, id, mass, x, y, z, vx, vy, vz)   every body every time step.

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ my_universe <- create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = 5.97e24) |>   add_body("Moon", mass = 7.34e22, x = 3.84e8, vy = 1022) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) } # }
